message.content:
OpenClaw’s memory system is actually pretty explicit and file-first — it’s not doing any hidden “scan your Obsidian vault and inject it” magic.

🧠 How OpenClaw maintains history & context

It uses local Markdown files + targeted retrieval, not a passive global scrape.

1. Primary memory sources (local files)

Everything lives in your workspace:

memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md → daily logs (short-term memory)

MEMORY.md → curated long-term memory

Other docs (USER.md, SOUL.md, etc.) → identity + preferences

These are the source of truth. No database, no vector store unless you add one yourself.

2. Retrieval-first behavior (important)

OpenClaw does not blindly load everything into the prompt.

Instead it follows this pattern:

When a question involves past context → it must call memory_search

That searches across:

MEMORY.md

memory/*.md

(optionally past session transcripts)

Then it uses memory_get to pull only the relevant lines

👉 So it’s selective recall, not full context dumping.

3. What gets sent to the AI model

Yes — retrieved memory does get injected into the prompt, but:

Only relevant snippets, not whole files

Only when needed (not every message)

Keeps context small and focused

This is how it “enhances” responses without blowing up token usage.

4. What happens on every session start

Per the system rules:

It automatically reads:

SOUL.md (personality)

USER.md (about you)

Today + yesterday’s memory files

MEMORY.md (only in direct/private chats)

So there’s always a baseline context, even before retrieval.

❌ What it does not do (by default)

❌ It does not scan your whole filesystem

❌ It does not index Obsidian automatically

❌ It does not inject all markdown files into prompts

❌ It does not maintain hidden long chat history outside files

If you want Obsidian integration, you’d have to:

sync your vault into the workspace, or

build/use a skill that queries it

🧩 Big picture

Think of it like:

Filesystem = brain

memory_search = recall

memory_get = focusing on the exact memory

prompt = working memory

⚡ Subtle but important detail

This line in your dataset matters a lot:

“Memory is limited — if you want to remember something, WRITE IT TO A FILE”

That’s literal. If it’s not written:
➡️ it does not persist
➡️ it will not be recalled later

Bottom line

OpenClaw uses local markdown files as memory

It retrieves context on demand, not preloading everything

It injects only relevant snippets into prompts

It does not automatically use Obsidian or external notes unless you wire that up

If you want, I can show you how to turn this into a much more powerful system (e.g., Obsidian + semantic search + auto-linking), which is where things get really interesting.
